name: Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail
name_original: Entre el río Eria y el arroyo de la Almuceira — Benavente y los Valles
description: This moderate 22.6-mile touring cycling route explores the Benavente y los Valles region, featuring riverside forests and historical sites.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Castrocalbón features a landscape shaped by the Río Ería, which creates fertile river plains ideal for scenic rides. The region also includes arid alluvial terraces and gentle hills, providing varied terrain with gentle climbs and descents. Historical elements, such as sections of the ancient Vía XVII Roman road, offer unique routes through the municipality. This blend of natural and historical features provides diverse options for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Castrocalbón The most popular touring cycling route is Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River, a 46.2 miles (74.4 km) trail that takes 5 hours 6 minutes to complete. This difficult route explores the Eria River valley and historical sites. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail, a moderate 22.6 miles (36.3 km) path. This route offers a mix of valley scenery and historical points of interest. Local touring cyclists also love the Santiago de Millas – Destriana Village Well loop from Villamontán de la Valduerna, a 22.0 miles (35.4 km) trail leading through the Valduerna region, often completed in about 2 hours 7 minutes. Touring cycling around Castrocalbón is defined by fertile river plains, gentle hills, and historical Roman roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to more challenging routes. The routes in Castrocalbón are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 24 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castrocalbón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Castrocalbón?
A: There are over 10 touring cycling routes available around Castrocalbón, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes around Castrocalbón?
A: The terrain around Castrocalbón is quite varied. You'll find pleasant, relatively flat routes along the fertile river plains of the Río Ería, as well as routes that traverse arid alluvial terraces and gentle hills. These elevated areas offer gentle climbs and descents, providing diverse cycling experiences.

Q: What historical landmarks can I explore while cycling in Castrocalbón?
A: Castrocalbón is rich in history. You can cycle along sections of the ancient &lt;strong&gt;Vía XVII Roman road&lt;/strong&gt;, particularly well-preserved in the area of La Chana. Other historical points of interest include the ruins of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5510280" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Castillo&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5786385" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iglesia del Salvador&lt;/a&gt; with its Mozarabic brickwork, and an old hermitage near the castle.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly touring cycling routes in Castrocalbón?
A: Yes, there is at least one easy touring cycling route. For a relaxed ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38159824" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Duerna River loop from Villamontán de la Valduerna&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 10.3 miles (16.6 km) long and features minimal elevation changes.

Q: Are there more challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 4 difficult routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1037602481/roman-camp-of-petavonium-from-castrocontrigo-circular-by-the-eria-river" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Camp of Petavonium from Castrocontrigo - circular by the Eria River&lt;/a&gt;, a 46.2 miles (74.4 km) trail that explores the Eria River valley and historical sites.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Castrocalbón?
A: The region benefits from a pleasant climate due to its abundant flora and fresh waters, even when other parts of the high plateau experience more extreme conditions. This suggests that spring and autumn would likely be ideal, offering comfortable temperatures for cycling while avoiding the peak heat of summer or potential chill of winter.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Castrocalbón?
A: Yes, several routes are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31522504" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santiago de Millas – Destriana Village Well loop from Villamontán de la Valduerna&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option, covering 22.0 miles (35.4 km) through the Valduerna region.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy about cycling in Castrocalbón?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the blend of natural beauty, such as the fertile river plains, and the unique historical elements like the ancient Roman roads and castle ruins that can be explored along the routes.

Q: Are there any specific viewpoints or natural features to look out for on the routes?
A: Yes, the routes often follow the &lt;strong&gt;Río Ería&lt;/strong&gt;, offering scenic views of its wooded river plains. The higher, arid alluvial terraces and gentle hills provide expansive views of the surrounding countryside. While not explicitly named as viewpoints, the varied topography ensures picturesque vistas throughout your ride.

Q: Can I find routes that combine natural scenery with historical sites?
A: Many routes in Castrocalbón offer this combination. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383958335/vidriales-valley-and-petavonium-camp-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 22.6 miles (36.3 km) path that provides a mix of beautiful valley scenery and significant historical points of interest, including the ancient Roman camp.

Q: Are there any other castles or historical sites nearby that I can visit?
A: Beyond the local castle ruins, you can explore other historical sites in the vicinity. Notable examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5786385" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de los Pimentel&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5553938" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Villanueva de Jamuz&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6039414" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castrocontrigo Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6352862" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of the Royal Monastery of Santa María de Nogales&lt;/a&gt; also offer a glimpse into the region's past.

Q: What is the average length of touring cycling routes in Castrocalbón?
A: The routes vary in length, but many fall within a moderate distance. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38160894" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bike loop from Nogarejas&lt;/a&gt; is about 16.0 miles (25.8 km), while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1383958335/vidriales-valley-and-petavonium-camp-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidriales Valley and Petavonium Camp Trail&lt;/a&gt; is 22.6 miles (36.3 km). There are also longer options for those seeking extended rides.
tips: There are several theories about the peculiar name of this Castro: some suggest that it comes from the lime that was manufactured here; others if it was founded by a member of the Calvo Roman family; It is also thought that it may be due to the oak-based charcoal that was produced in its vicinity, and there are those who think that the origin of the name is, on the contrary, Mozarabic.
An archaeological site ruins the Hermitage and the Palace located in the valley of the Eria river. Few remains of the palace apart from a restored Gothic arch.

An archaeological site of great importance, the Roman Camp of Petavonium is a vestige of the Roman Empire in the Iberian Peninsula. The camp was a fortified outpost protecting trade routes in the area.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for touring cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route offers a varied terrain typical of the Benavente y los Valles region. You can expect a mix of paved roads, unpaved sections, and potentially some dirt tracks or forest paths. While much of the route is suitable for touring bicycles, some segments might be rougher.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You will experience diverse landscapes, including abundant riverside forests along the Eria river. The region is known for its green meadows, and you may pass through areas with dense forests and traditional villages, offering picturesque views and a connection with nature.

faqs.question: What cultural or historical points of interest are on the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes by several notable historical sites. You can see the Eria Bridge in Castrocalbón, the Gothic Arch of the Palace of Castrocalbón, and the Petavonium Roman Camp Ruins.

faqs.question: Is the route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate for touring cycling. While it's not considered a beginner trail, cyclists with good fitness and some experience on varied terrain should find it manageable. Some sections might require more effort due to unpaved surfaces or slight inclines.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle in the Benavente y los Valles region is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) when the weather is milder and more pleasant for cycling. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring colder temperatures and potentially less favorable conditions.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on this touring bicycle route, it's always advisable to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through villages, near livestock, or on busier sections. Be mindful of other cyclists and local regulations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths in the Benavente y los Valles region.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: As this is a touring bicycle route, you can typically find parking in the towns or villages along the route, such as Benavente or Castrocalbón. It's recommended to check local parking availability in your chosen starting point.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, small sections of this route overlap with other local paths. Specifically, it briefly passes through parts of the Puente de Hierro and Calle El Caño.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle trip on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's moderate difficulty and varied terrain, it's recommended to bring a well-maintained touring bicycle, a helmet, sufficient water, snacks, a basic repair kit, and appropriate clothing for the weather. Sun protection is also advisable.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is primarily natural, you will likely pass through or near villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, or shops to refuel. It's a good idea to carry some snacks and water, especially for longer stretches between towns.

waypoints.name: Gothic Arch of the Palace of Castrocalbón
